1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:00,770
Hello all.

2
00:00:00,770 --> 00:00:03,200
Welcome to another session of Laubins.

3
00:00:03,200 --> 00:00:09,440
In this session we'll be knowing more about PowerShell, which is an important role that the threat

4
00:00:09,440 --> 00:00:13,550
actors usually use in all their attack patterns.

5
00:00:13,550 --> 00:00:20,660
So to touch base with what is PowerShell, it's a framework developed by Microsoft and it is based on

6
00:00:20,940 --> 00:00:22,370
Dotnet framework.

7
00:00:22,400 --> 00:00:28,970
It is a inbuilt application which has a console exactly similar to that of Cmd.exe.

8
00:00:29,060 --> 00:00:35,960
So basically it's a scripting language that is developed by Microsoft to actually automate tasks on

9
00:00:35,960 --> 00:00:37,700
Windows machines.

10
00:00:37,700 --> 00:00:44,630
So this PowerShell has always been a major laubin that's been used by the threat actors.

11
00:00:44,630 --> 00:00:51,890
So let me let me teach you, how are these professionals being abused by the threat actors?

12
00:00:51,890 --> 00:00:59,600
So initially the first point will be they use a malicious code to get executed into the victim's machine.

13
00:00:59,600 --> 00:01:06,930
So basically they develop their malware or the threat, uh, what they have, what they have scripted

14
00:01:06,930 --> 00:01:12,540
in the form of a code, and they basically execute it in the form of a script in PowerShell dot exe.

15
00:01:12,750 --> 00:01:19,200
And they also use, they also use PowerShell to steal data from the victim's machine.

16
00:01:19,290 --> 00:01:26,100
Stealing data does not only mean, uh, like getting the passwords of the victim, getting the documents

17
00:01:26,100 --> 00:01:29,970
of the victim, it also means like knowing more about the victim's mission.

18
00:01:29,970 --> 00:01:35,400
For example, there is a process, like there is a command in PowerShell called Get Process, which

19
00:01:35,400 --> 00:01:40,140
is used to list all the running processes that are currently being executed on the machine.

20
00:01:40,140 --> 00:01:47,490
This will make the threat actors know what are the softwares that can be leveraged in the near future.

21
00:01:47,490 --> 00:01:53,010
For example, there is a software called three C that had a vulnerability recently.

22
00:01:53,010 --> 00:01:59,040
So on listing the process, if the threat actor saw a process of three being running on the machine,

23
00:01:59,040 --> 00:02:06,030
it would be easy for him to know that they are running a vulnerable version and they can easily leverage

24
00:02:06,030 --> 00:02:09,000
it or they can do exploit out of the vulnerability.

25
00:02:09,030 --> 00:02:09,330
Right.

26
00:02:09,330 --> 00:02:15,300
And they can also get more details about a process that is currently running.

27
00:02:15,300 --> 00:02:20,430
For example, they may know about the version, the supporting files, etcetera.

28
00:02:20,430 --> 00:02:26,640
So knowing more about the version also gives them a clue about the vulnerable versions a software might

29
00:02:26,640 --> 00:02:27,480
be running.

30
00:02:27,930 --> 00:02:34,140
These are the ways and the next thing is like it can be used to install a malware inside the system

31
00:02:34,140 --> 00:02:35,010
in the same way.

32
00:02:35,010 --> 00:02:38,280
Like they develop a code and they execute it in the form of a script.

33
00:02:38,280 --> 00:02:45,570
And this script can also be used to spread the particular malware throughout their network in the state.

34
00:02:45,840 --> 00:02:46,040
Yeah.

35
00:02:46,650 --> 00:02:51,930
And in few cases we have also seen threat actors using PowerShell to terminate a process.

36
00:02:51,930 --> 00:02:58,470
So there are many cases where threat actor does not want it a process to remain in the system because

37
00:02:58,470 --> 00:03:01,350
they might get identified by various defense tools.

38
00:03:01,350 --> 00:03:04,380
So they actually terminate those processes.

39
00:03:05,010 --> 00:03:05,550
Yeah.

40
00:03:05,550 --> 00:03:06,930
To uh, yeah.

41
00:03:06,930 --> 00:03:10,380
To escape from the errors, the Avs, etcetera.

42
00:03:10,380 --> 00:03:13,260
So what is the syntax of PowerShell?

43
00:03:13,800 --> 00:03:15,630
Yeah, the syntax is simple.

44
00:03:15,630 --> 00:03:18,030
The PowerShell and the file name.

45
00:03:18,030 --> 00:03:20,970
The file name is basically the script that we are going to execute.

46
00:03:20,970 --> 00:03:27,390
PowerShell is also a very easy, human friendly language where it accepts any verbal form of comments

47
00:03:27,690 --> 00:03:33,690
as told get process or any verbs that you can give for your intent to get the results.

48
00:03:33,690 --> 00:03:38,310
And you can also use the PowerShell help command that will give you the list of commands that you can

49
00:03:38,310 --> 00:03:39,270
use in the future.

50
00:03:39,270 --> 00:03:45,630
So as in my PowerShell comes under the tactics, execution and technique, command and scripting.

51
00:03:46,970 --> 00:03:47,920
Interpreter.

52
00:03:47,930 --> 00:03:53,320
So the next thing is like, what part of PowerShell basically attracts the threat actors?

53
00:03:53,330 --> 00:04:00,320
So through PowerShell you can simultaneously administer all Windows application, right from Active

54
00:04:00,320 --> 00:04:05,630
Directory, DNS exchange servers, Hyper-V file servers and whatnot.

55
00:04:05,630 --> 00:04:14,420
So this gives the threat actor the complete configuration of the victim's machine, which can be leveraged

56
00:04:14,420 --> 00:04:18,200
or which can be exploited in the future attack.

57
00:04:18,230 --> 00:04:21,440
So these are the ways like the threat actors use.

58
00:04:21,440 --> 00:04:26,930
And in the next slide, I have given two ways in which the PowerShell gets abused.

59
00:04:26,930 --> 00:04:34,340
So the one in the left hand side, I have given us a normal screenshot where a script called my first

60
00:04:34,340 --> 00:04:37,430
script dot PS1 getting executed into the machine.

61
00:04:37,430 --> 00:04:44,930
This is a simple way where the threat actors copy the code, the malicious malware or their code and

62
00:04:44,930 --> 00:04:49,740
they execute and they save it in a form of a script and execute it in PowerShell.

63
00:04:49,740 --> 00:04:55,680
In the next screenshot I have given a code which is being obfuscated and it is being executed on the

64
00:04:55,680 --> 00:04:56,430
victim machine.

65
00:04:56,430 --> 00:05:04,350
So usually threat actor, usually nowadays the are smart and are smart as they terminate the PowerShell

66
00:05:04,350 --> 00:05:08,820
when it is being directly malicious PowerShell scripts that is being directly executed.

67
00:05:08,820 --> 00:05:15,270
So threat actors use various decoding methods to obfuscate their script and later execute on the machine.

68
00:05:15,270 --> 00:05:21,270
So this obfuscated code also can contain any malicious intent where they can have a C2 that connects

69
00:05:21,270 --> 00:05:22,770
to their cobalt strike beacon.

70
00:05:22,800 --> 00:05:28,080
They can download a malicious file inside the system or whatever their intent will be.

71
00:05:28,080 --> 00:05:33,870
So these are the there are many such ways that actors execute a PowerShell script.

72
00:05:33,900 --> 00:05:38,160
This is all about PowerShell being used as a weapon.

73
00:05:38,190 --> 00:05:39,640
Thank you for watching the video.

74
00:05:39,660 --> 00:05:40,620
Happy Learning.

00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:02,900

00:00:02,930 --> 00:00:09,470

3
00:00:09,470 --> 00:00:13,400
So before going into MC, let us discuss what is WMI.

4
00:00:13,610 --> 00:00:16,580
WMI which stands for Windows Management.

5
00:00:16,580 --> 00:00:24,050
Instrumentation is a set of rules, commands or specifications that are designed by the Microsoft team,

6
00:00:24,140 --> 00:00:32,240
which can be easily utilized to manage all the devices inside the network and the application that those

7
00:00:32,240 --> 00:00:37,460
devices are using inside the network from Windows Computing system.

8
00:00:37,460 --> 00:00:45,200
So in other words, we can say WMI just provides the user with all the information about the status

9
00:00:45,200 --> 00:00:46,820
of the computer.

10
00:00:46,820 --> 00:00:52,070
It can be a local or a remote computer that are managed within a network.

11
00:00:52,070 --> 00:00:53,850
So what is WMC?

12
00:00:53,990 --> 00:01:01,160
So how can these commands or specifications can be utilized by a by the user?

13
00:01:01,160 --> 00:01:08,570
So the utility called WMI, which is Windows Management Instrumentation Command line, comes into the

14
00:01:08,570 --> 00:01:09,080
picture.

15
00:01:09,080 --> 00:01:14,090
So this is just the user interruption of those rules and comments.

16
00:01:14,090 --> 00:01:17,750
So this actually these rules are being made.

17
00:01:18,050 --> 00:01:25,850
I mean, the WMI is being made for the high end users such as the system administrators.

18
00:01:25,850 --> 00:01:32,360
So to easily communicate with the machines that are inside the network.

19
00:01:32,360 --> 00:01:38,540
So I will also say the WMC, so WMC can be used to do anything.

20
00:01:38,540 --> 00:01:44,000
So what makes it special is that it can be used for persistence.

21
00:01:44,000 --> 00:01:50,360

22
00:01:50,360 --> 00:01:52,160

23
00:01:52,160 --> 00:02:00,050
But sometimes what happens is there are many defensive technology that terminates the Bitcoin that the

24

00:02:01,950 --> 00:02:06,960

26
00:02:06,960 --> 00:02:11,550

27
00:02:11,550 --> 00:02:19,590
For him to persist inside the network of the victim even though his bitcoin is being terminated.

28
00:02:19,590 --> 00:02:24,780
So how can be utilize so WMC let me.

29
00:02:25,050 --> 00:02:31,260
This session is being practically explained in the upcoming sessions.

30
00:02:31,260 --> 00:02:33,150
We will see more about it in the future.

31
00:02:33,150 --> 00:02:35,070

32
00:02:35,070 --> 00:02:42,630
So WMC, the command, after executing a command, you can also give a condition to where that can be

33
00:02:42,630 --> 00:02:43,050
used.

34
00:02:43,620 --> 00:02:53,120
You can give a command to WMC telling execute this malicious command when the user opens notepad.exe

35
00:02:53,580 --> 00:03:02,070
or when a user opens calculator or or any daily utility you can give so that the user will be clueless

36
00:03:02,070 --> 00:03:05,400
of why the malicious code is being run.

37
00:03:05,400 --> 00:03:12,000
Even if the initial bitcoin or the initial attacker's machine connection is being terminated.

38
00:03:12,000 --> 00:03:13,530
So this is how it can be used.

39
00:03:13,530 --> 00:03:21,150
And next point I wanted to discuss is what makes it interesting for threat actor to utilize or to leverage

40
00:03:21,160 --> 00:03:22,020
WMC.

41
00:03:22,200 --> 00:03:30,930
So the first point will be it is a powerful Windows utility that allows user to control and manage Windows

42
00:03:30,930 --> 00:03:32,580
system from command line.

43
00:03:32,580 --> 00:03:42,480
So all the systems that is managed inside the network can be easily can be easily queried or can the

44
00:03:42,480 --> 00:03:48,990
information about all the machines can easily be taken using the command line with the help of WMI.

45
00:03:49,350 --> 00:03:51,660
I will explain you the comments that can be used.

46
00:03:51,660 --> 00:03:58,350
And the next point is it is already a inbuilt utility, so they need not be any downloading of a special

47
00:03:58,380 --> 00:04:05,640
tool or importing of any package to use this command because that can be easily visible in hands of

48
00:04:05,640 --> 00:04:08,790
the defensing tools or the monitoring tools.

49
00:04:08,790 --> 00:04:11,990
So it is already inbuilt, so it can be easily leveraged.

50
00:04:12,000 --> 00:04:19,140
Second thing, WMI has a very wide range of comments that can be utilized, can be easily leveraged

51
00:04:19,140 --> 00:04:21,660
to get more information about the devices.

52
00:04:21,660 --> 00:04:25,680
So the next point is that it has a wide range of vulnerabilities.

53
00:04:25,680 --> 00:04:32,160
I mean, vulnerabilities because this is basically designed for system administrators to make their

54
00:04:32,160 --> 00:04:38,340
work simple, to do any configuration or to do any management of devices that are present inside the

55

56
00:04:38,970 --> 00:04:43,290
So they have a complete control over the network.

57
00:04:43,290 --> 00:04:51,660

58
00:04:51,660 --> 00:04:52,530
easily utilized.

59
00:04:52,530 --> 00:04:54,660

60
00:04:54,660 --> 00:04:56,880

61
00:04:57,510 --> 00:04:58,890
The first comment is.

62
00:05:00,040 --> 00:05:01,820
A process called create.

63
00:05:01,820 --> 00:05:05,460
Kelsey This is a simple command that is used to execute.

64
00:05:05,480 --> 00:05:09,440
Kelsey dot x So this Kelsey dot x can be.

65
00:05:10,580 --> 00:05:14,120
Replace with any malicious x that is already been in.

66
00:05:14,360 --> 00:05:17,360
It can be the payload of the threat actor or anything.

67
00:05:17,360 --> 00:05:23,040
So the second command is like WMI C dot x slash node colon.

68
00:05:23,060 --> 00:05:24,920
That is to address the IP address.

69
00:05:24,920 --> 00:05:30,380
So this IP address means the IP address of the victim's machine, the local machine's IP address and

70
00:05:30,380 --> 00:05:33,350
servers where caption like SQL Server.

71
00:05:33,350 --> 00:05:41,450
So this literally means it will give the detail if SQL Server is being currently used inside the system.

72
00:05:41,510 --> 00:05:42,200
SQL Server.

73
00:05:42,200 --> 00:05:48,470
So once SQL Server is being identified, we can later go into finding the versions.

74
00:05:48,470 --> 00:05:55,640
If it is vulnerable, it's an easy exploit so the threat actor will can take leverage of it, right?

75
00:05:55,640 --> 00:05:58,280
So it can be replaced by any application.

76
00:05:58,280 --> 00:06:04,550
It can be the exchange server or any vulnerability that is currently present inside the environment.

77
00:06:04,550 --> 00:06:12,930
I would also like to mention WannaCry, the most popular ransomware group once used volume Shadow copy

78
00:06:12,960 --> 00:06:15,180

79
00:06:15,390 --> 00:06:19,350

81

00:06:21,330 --> 00:06:27,090

00:06:27,090 --> 00:06:35,580

# Section 4: MITRE ATT&CK® framework discussion

1
00:00:00,230 --> 00:00:00,920
Hello, all.

2
00:00:00,920 --> 00:00:02,630
Welcome to another session.

3
00:00:02,630 --> 00:00:06,830
In this session, we will be knowing more about Miter and its origin.

4
00:00:06,830 --> 00:00:13,220
So before knowing about miter, the origin of miter is nothing but the cyber kill chain.

5
00:00:13,220 --> 00:00:21,950
So in the cyber kill chain, which was deployed developed by Lockheed Martin, who was a member of US

6
00:00:21,950 --> 00:00:22,760
military.

7
00:00:22,760 --> 00:00:31,490
So they have they wanted a pattern by which they could launch a successful attack.

8
00:00:31,520 --> 00:00:35,750
That is where the concept of cyber kill chain came into existence.

9
00:00:35,750 --> 00:00:40,250
Their initial idea was to identify and prevent attack.

10
00:00:40,250 --> 00:00:43,130
So this gave birth to the cyber kill chain.

11
00:00:43,130 --> 00:00:51,200
And in cyber kill chain, if you see there are seven different concepts stages, not concepts that with

12
00:00:51,200 --> 00:00:58,700
the initially starting with reconnaissance, weaponization, delivery, exploitation, installation,

13
00:00:58,700 --> 00:01:01,190
command and control, actions and objective.

14
00:01:01,200 --> 00:01:09,690
So if you see these terminologies this more more or like it is more appropriate to a military terminology

15
00:01:09,690 --> 00:01:11,280
than to a cyber terminology.

16
00:01:11,280 --> 00:01:15,960
So yeah, because this is built for their own attack pattern, right?

17
00:01:15,960 --> 00:01:24,030
So it was there and this is the intelligent driven defense model that is being published years back.

18
00:01:24,030 --> 00:01:32,280
So this gave inspiration for a group called Miter Attack Organization who are the non-profitable groups,

19
00:01:32,280 --> 00:01:37,470
and they developed 14 different stages of this seven stages.

20
00:01:37,470 --> 00:01:40,530
So I'll explain in detail about the miter.

21
00:01:40,530 --> 00:01:43,170
And this is all about the cyber kill chain.

22
00:01:43,740 --> 00:01:51,600
So this is the miter attack, their official page where you could see their matrix and many more tabs

23
00:01:51,600 --> 00:01:52,350
to explore.

24
00:01:52,380 --> 00:01:55,440
Yeah, we can explore maximum tabs as possible.

25
00:01:55,440 --> 00:01:55,770
Yeah.

26
00:01:55,770 --> 00:01:59,760
This is the URL that is attack miter.org.

27
00:01:59,910 --> 00:02:07,950
And if you scroll down you can see the miter tactics and their subsequent techniques and their 14 different

28
00:02:07,950 --> 00:02:10,020
stages in one matrix.

29
00:02:10,020 --> 00:02:10,440
Yeah.

30
00:02:10,440 --> 00:02:12,360
I will define more about yeah.

31
00:02:12,420 --> 00:02:12,930
Miter.

32
00:02:13,080 --> 00:02:21,480
So before going into all these stages, let us discuss what is the need to learn miter.

33
00:02:21,480 --> 00:02:29,340
So whenever we attend a interview, whenever we go for a certification or we go for anything in a cyber

34
00:02:29,340 --> 00:02:35,730
new chapter of cyber security, this miter plays a very vital role under everything.

35
00:02:35,730 --> 00:02:38,190
So their expectations are more of miter.

36
00:02:38,190 --> 00:02:38,730
So what?

37
00:02:38,730 --> 00:02:40,170
Why should we learn miter?

38
00:02:40,170 --> 00:02:51,540
So initially miter is a very important, crucial thing to mean, a concept to understand, because nowadays

39
00:02:51,540 --> 00:02:54,240
there is a pattern of all the attackers.

40
00:02:54,240 --> 00:03:00,960
So if you take a group of ransomware or any malware, everything has a patterns.

41
00:03:00,960 --> 00:03:07,530
It's not like they come, they launch, and whichever is possible, it's not a trial and error method.

42
00:03:07,530 --> 00:03:15,150
It's always a predefined attack pattern and every group follows a similar pattern.

43
00:03:15,150 --> 00:03:22,370
So that is what has been identified by miter and they have framed it into the stages.

44
00:03:22,380 --> 00:03:30,030
So being in a defensive team, so we face malware day to day, we face attacks day to day.

45
00:03:30,030 --> 00:03:37,500
So whenever we see, for example, I am doing a defense for a particular estate for a particular company

46
00:03:37,500 --> 00:03:43,650
and I'm seeing a malware being detected by an antivirus, We all have all the defensive technologies.

47
00:03:43,650 --> 00:03:55,080
So if I see a malware being detected by an antivirus, so as a defensive analyst, what I do is if I

48
00:03:55,080 --> 00:03:59,400
don't have any clue about the malware, so I leave it, okay, it got detected, it got deleted, it's

49
00:03:59,400 --> 00:04:00,150
not the thing.

50
00:04:00,150 --> 00:04:06,750
So all malware like the whenever it is intended to put into an estate, everybody knows it will get

51
00:04:06,750 --> 00:04:10,200
detected in with any of the defensive tools.

52
00:04:10,200 --> 00:04:10,440
Right.

53
00:04:10,440 --> 00:04:15,120
So and our first goal will be what is the next step of the malware?

54
00:04:15,150 --> 00:04:17,160
What is its intent to do?

55
00:04:17,160 --> 00:04:26,790
So if I know the pattern of which the malware is trying to move inside the system or trying to do how

56
00:04:26,790 --> 00:04:32,640
it is, what it is trying to exploit all this concept, if I know properly, I can stop the malware.

57
00:04:32,640 --> 00:04:36,990
So there are few malwares that get directed and they get deleted.

58
00:04:36,990 --> 00:04:40,980
But it's not that there will be malicious that will be loaded in memory.

59
00:04:40,980 --> 00:04:44,670
There will be malicious persistence that will be created on disk.

60
00:04:44,670 --> 00:04:51,240
So this is all like only if you identify the attack pattern of a malware, you will be very conscious

61
00:04:51,240 --> 00:04:53,340
about stopping it.

62
00:04:53,340 --> 00:04:56,040
And the next thing is how it came into the system.

63
00:04:56,040 --> 00:04:59,790
You also have to identify it, how it came to the system just to stop other.

64
00:04:59,930 --> 00:05:01,940
Always getting deployed easily, right?

65
00:05:01,940 --> 00:05:03,200
So this is how it works.

66
00:05:03,200 --> 00:05:08,830
For example, if you take a quackpot, for example, you all would have heard about the malware Quackpot.

67
00:05:08,840 --> 00:05:12,860
So even Quackpot has a very, very similar pattern.

68
00:05:12,860 --> 00:05:16,100
So see, Quackpot is identified only with its pattern.

69
00:05:16,100 --> 00:05:23,030
So a ransomware is group is being identified only with their pattern, how they do all the process,

70
00:05:23,030 --> 00:05:25,640
all this attack process inside the environment.

71
00:05:25,640 --> 00:05:28,130
So if you take a quackpot, it is simple.

72
00:05:28,130 --> 00:05:32,930
It just comes through the phishing email, uh, through any emailing sources.

73
00:05:32,960 --> 00:05:35,780
Uh, can it be outlook exchange anything?

74
00:05:35,780 --> 00:05:45,540
It comes uh, through the email and the email will download a dot HTML file and if you open the HTML

75
00:05:45,560 --> 00:05:54,500
file it will give you a zip file from which a Json file gets downloaded like unzip it will get zip file

76
00:05:54,500 --> 00:06:02,790
will get unzipped into a dot js file and this JS file will directly communicate with the quackpot c2.

77
00:06:02,820 --> 00:06:05,280
This is the pattern of Quackpot.

78
00:06:05,280 --> 00:06:06,990
So I will say this.

79
00:06:07,140 --> 00:06:10,470
This is the pattern of Jason's sorry pattern of Quackpot.

80
00:06:10,470 --> 00:06:16,440
So whenever you see a zip file and out of this zip file, a JS file is coming out and it's connecting

81
00:06:16,470 --> 00:06:17,010
to a C2.

82
00:06:17,040 --> 00:06:17,520
Definitely.

83
00:06:17,520 --> 00:06:18,090
It's a Quackpot.

84
00:06:18,090 --> 00:06:22,230
So every malware has their pattern, take it and import it.

85
00:06:22,260 --> 00:06:23,190
So ghoulish.

86
00:06:23,190 --> 00:06:29,070
So any malware has its own pattern and it is being identified only because of their pattern.

87
00:06:29,100 --> 00:06:30,810
This is the basic understanding.

88
00:06:30,810 --> 00:06:37,620
So if you identify the pattern, you can stop their stages, you can you can know what is the next stage

89
00:06:37,620 --> 00:06:38,100
of it.

90
00:06:38,100 --> 00:06:45,090
So it it is a good boon to the cyber security defensive analysts because it has a pattern.

91
00:06:45,090 --> 00:06:53,370
So if you identify it in a middle stage, uh, before something bad is being connected or is bad, bad

92
00:06:53,370 --> 00:06:56,630
is being taken from the environment, you can stop it.

93
00:06:56,640 --> 00:06:59,250
This is the basic understanding of miter.

94
00:06:59,250 --> 00:07:04,890
And now we will explore about each stages of miter.

95
00:07:06,040 --> 00:07:06,930
Matrix.

96
00:07:06,940 --> 00:07:16,830
So there are 14 these seven different stages are being converted into 14 different tactics called.

97
00:07:16,840 --> 00:07:21,580
So these tactics are the block letters that is given over here.

98
00:07:21,610 --> 00:07:26,410
These blue color block letters are the tactics and they have techniques.

99
00:07:26,410 --> 00:07:27,880
So what is this tactics?

100
00:07:27,910 --> 00:07:33,490
Tactics are the stages of which a attack is being followed.

101
00:07:33,580 --> 00:07:33,850
Okay.

102
00:07:33,850 --> 00:07:38,920
By which the attack is being followed and techniques are like inside a tactic.

103
00:07:38,950 --> 00:07:40,300
You have multiple techniques.

See, it's not like only one way, right?

105

It can be multiple ways for same tactic can happen, right?

106
00:07:47,470 --> 00:07:49,990
So in that way it is like different.

The techniques are not constant.

108

# Section 18: History of Ransomwares

1
00:00:00,530 --> 00:00:01,280
Hello, all.

2
00:00:01,280 --> 00:00:03,720
Welcome to a new session on ransomware.

3
00:00:03,740 --> 00:00:07,370
In this session, we'll be knowing more about ransomware and its type.

4
00:00:07,370 --> 00:00:13,520
And also in particular, we will be talking about three different ransomware, namely the Akira Rook

5
00:00:13,520 --> 00:00:22,310
and Lockbit and about the attack flow, how it how it is being deployed into the system undergoing a

6
00:00:22,310 --> 00:00:23,490
successful attack.

7
00:00:23,510 --> 00:00:30,970
So before going into the particular ransomware groups, let us know more about ransomware.

8
00:00:30,980 --> 00:00:41,510
So ransomware is the most critical and a happening malware that shakes up the world as it encrypts as

9
00:00:41,510 --> 00:00:46,670
it deploys the encryption to hold a victim's information for a ransom.

10
00:00:47,540 --> 00:00:54,650
So, as you all know, the organization's critical data will be encrypted, restricting them from accessing

11
00:00:54,650 --> 00:00:55,060
it.

12
00:00:55,070 --> 00:01:04,130
So how the information about the attack is being like delivered to the organization is in the means

13
00:01:04,130 --> 00:01:05,110
of a ransom note.

14
00:01:05,120 --> 00:01:06,840
I'll be showing that in the next slide.

15
00:01:06,860 --> 00:01:13,790
Like ransom note is being like deployed onto the organization's mission.

16
00:01:13,790 --> 00:01:21,650
And this appears each time the user tries to access a file or a application that is being completely

17
00:01:21,650 --> 00:01:23,810
encrypted by the ransomware.

18
00:01:23,840 --> 00:01:24,290
Yeah.

19
00:01:24,290 --> 00:01:32,930
So this is how a ransomware like it is, how we can identify a ransomware encryption onto the system.

20
00:01:32,930 --> 00:01:36,130
So now we will discuss about the types of ransomware.

21
00:01:36,140 --> 00:01:44,330
So I have given the top, I'm going to discuss about the top three ransomware, which I have basically

22
00:01:44,330 --> 00:01:51,110
classified based on the behavior, how it works and on which what it focuses mainly on.

23
00:01:51,110 --> 00:01:53,840
So the first one is the supply chain ransomware.

24
00:01:54,110 --> 00:02:02,940
So in this, as we already discussed about the supply chain attack, this ransomware targets on organization

25
00:02:02,940 --> 00:02:05,100
that uses a third party vendor.

26
00:02:05,100 --> 00:02:06,220
So this third party.

27
00:02:06,240 --> 00:02:10,470
So there are multiple organizations that uses a common third party vendor.

28
00:02:10,470 --> 00:02:17,580
And this ransomware group that is the supply chain ransomware group will target those third party vendors.

29
00:02:17,580 --> 00:02:27,870
So in this what happens is like a particular malicious tool or the software will get deployed while

30
00:02:28,290 --> 00:02:36,330
be while through the third party vendor as an update is being formulated through the third party vendor

31
00:02:36,330 --> 00:02:42,660
to the organization or any patching that is being conducted by the third party vendor, uh, on the

32
00:02:42,930 --> 00:02:44,460
like on the organizations.

33
00:02:44,460 --> 00:02:46,710
So it will be deployed through that.

34
00:02:46,710 --> 00:02:53,850
So it will be so the malicious software will be in embedded in those vendors patch or updates.

35
00:02:53,850 --> 00:02:56,400
This is how it will get delivered.

36
00:02:56,400 --> 00:03:01,170
So the second type of ransomware is the double extortion ransomware.

37
00:03:01,170 --> 00:03:05,130
So this type of ransomware just aims to damage your reputation.

38
00:03:05,130 --> 00:03:12,180
I mean, the organization's reputation, like it encrypts steals and like the it will like, yeah,

39
00:03:12,180 --> 00:03:16,280
they threaten to publish the data onto the dark webs.

40
00:03:16,970 --> 00:03:22,200
So maybe to just to spoil the reputation of the organization.

41
00:03:22,200 --> 00:03:28,710
The third group is the ever trending group like it is the ransomware as a service group.

42
00:03:28,710 --> 00:03:35,760
So before if you take a ransomware are very hard to find because development, technical development

43
00:03:35,760 --> 00:03:42,000
of a malware was very hard for any intruders who wanted to attack an organization for any means for

44
00:03:42,000 --> 00:03:44,970
a ransom or for reputational damage for any means.

45
00:03:44,970 --> 00:03:50,640
But now there is an organization that is there are multiple organizations that provide ransomware as

46
00:03:50,640 --> 00:03:51,180
a service.

47
00:03:51,180 --> 00:03:59,250
So what happens is the cybercriminals can rent or lease those tools or ransomware.

48
00:03:59,270 --> 00:04:06,510
The ransomware that is the our vehicle, which goes like which goes as an attack one and to perform

49
00:04:06,510 --> 00:04:08,340
the proper attack pattern.

50
00:04:08,760 --> 00:04:16,650
So as we talked about this in the previous sessions, also these like it will be like a malware.

51
00:04:16,680 --> 00:04:22,410
So there are multiple groups that provide services like malware as a service is also being provided.

52
00:04:22,410 --> 00:04:28,170
So in this case it's the ransomware as a service group that gives you the complete ransomware package

53

60

61
00:05:09,550 --> 00:05:16,420
So how will you identify that your environment is being so you have identified that your environment

62
00:05:16,420 --> 00:05:23,170
is being encrypted or a ransomware is into your environment, but how do I know that Akira Ransomware

64

So that is how you can identify this through a extension called Dot Akira.

65
00:05:31,780 --> 00:05:38,140
In all the files that have been encrypted, files or applications or anything that have been encrypted

66

67
00:05:41,890 --> 00:05:49,840
So for example, if you take uh, like uh, notepad dot x or file name is Notepad dot x, it gets changed

68
00:05:49,840 --> 00:05:56,470
into Notepad dot x dot Akira that is the another extension gets added once the encryption is being done.

69
00:05:56,470 --> 00:06:05,410

70
00:06:05,410 --> 00:06:09,100
and the ransom note will be saved as f dot txt.

71
00:06:09,340 --> 00:06:17,680
So this is the sample of the ransom note that I have collected being mostly seen during an Akira group.

00:06:53,380 --> 00:06:55,240

through VPN, initial access is being happening.

dot is being leveraged for this method.

uh, cardinal value of SAS and it is being dumped.

And the one special feature of Akira is like usually when a credential dumping is being happening,

102

112
00:09:22,220 --> 00:09:23,060

113

The first one is like they are creating a scheduled task called Windows Update.

114
00:09:29,540 --> 00:09:34,550
And through this scheduled task, Discovery stage is being performed.

115
00:09:34,550 --> 00:09:42,290
So I have given a example pattern here where in this all the Discovery command, all the net commands

00:09:42,290 --> 00:09:45,560
for discovery is being stored in this dot bat file.

117
00:09:45,560 --> 00:09:53,420

118

119
00:09:56,540 --> 00:09:59,630
So once this bat file is being executed in.

120
00:09:59,740 --> 00:10:06,430
This particular directory, all the information about the program data directory will be stored under

121
00:10:06,430 --> 00:10:09,400
SVR dir dot txt.

122
00:10:09,640 --> 00:10:12,820
So this is how it collects all the information.

123
00:10:12,820 --> 00:10:15,700
Like about the files about the directory.

124
00:10:15,820 --> 00:10:20,320
Discovery is being happening through this schedule task and also it is being stored.

125
00:10:20,320 --> 00:10:24,430
The schedule task is being named with a legitimate name called Windows update.

126
00:10:24,790 --> 00:10:25,600
So this is done.

127
00:10:25,600 --> 00:10:29,510
And the second point is the advanced port scanner IP scanner.

128
00:10:29,530 --> 00:10:37,000
So if you are not familiar with this tool, it is a tool that is it's a free network scanning tool that

129
00:10:37,000 --> 00:10:43,270
it will allow you to quickly find open ports on the network computers inside the network.

00:10:43,270 --> 00:10:51,010

131
00:10:51,010 --> 00:10:51,340
ports.

00:10:53,170 --> 00:10:56,800

134
00:10:56,800 --> 00:11:02,690

135
00:11:02,690 --> 00:11:03,110
here.

136
00:11:03,500 --> 00:11:05,390
The next stage is the lateral movement.

137
00:11:05,390 --> 00:11:09,890
So after discovery, they have to laterally move inside the system.

138
00:11:09,890 --> 00:11:17,030
So it they also use the most famous lateral movement technique that is the RDP remote desktop protocol.

139
00:11:17,030 --> 00:11:23,660
So with this protocol over the Port 1149, there will be no restriction for the intruders once they

140
00:11:23,660 --> 00:11:27,530
use the RDP and they can move freely inside the network using the RDP.

141
00:11:27,530 --> 00:11:33,230
So it is the most common method that all the ransomware group use for two lateral movement.

142
00:11:33,230 --> 00:11:34,820
Yeah, this is how it happens.

143
00:11:34,820 --> 00:11:36,740
And next is the defense evasion.

144
00:11:36,740 --> 00:11:44,630
So on the defense evasion phase, it does not only disable the real time protection, it also creates

145
00:11:44,660 --> 00:11:45,680
a exclusion.

146
00:11:45,680 --> 00:11:51,800
As we we have discussed this in our previous sessions, like it creates a exclusion for the monitoring

147
00:11:51,830 --> 00:11:59,060
tools, for the defensive tools so that it can be it can survive without being detected.

148
00:11:59,060 --> 00:12:04,730
So yeah, usually what happens disabling of those monitoring tool happens.

149
00:12:04,730 --> 00:12:11,930
In this case, it adds an exclusion through the registry so that it they can easily escape being found

150
00:12:11,930 --> 00:12:15,620
by the other monitoring defensive tools.

151
00:12:15,950 --> 00:12:19,580
Yeah in this they have added an exclusion for Windows defender.

152
00:12:20,400 --> 00:12:20,640
Ya.

153
00:12:20,640 --> 00:12:23,040
The next stage is the command and control stage.

154
00:12:23,040 --> 00:12:25,410
So in this stage the.

00:12:25,440 --> 00:12:34,530
They are using the most popular tool, any desk tool for for them to provide a persistent remote access

156

into the affected organization.

157
00:12:36,150 --> 00:12:41,130
So on multiple systems to your as a C2 connection.

158
00:12:41,130 --> 00:12:48,150
So anydesk is the most common tool that and in this they have saved the anydesk in the init cache memory

159
00:12:48,150 --> 00:12:53,160
so that it goes unidentified so that no suspicious thing will come.

160
00:12:53,160 --> 00:12:56,490
Yeah, no suspicious detection will be seen later.

They are using a compromised account to download Winrar archiving software and they like zip all the

166

167

it out of the system.

168
00:13:30,810 --> 00:13:36,870
So this is what they are using a compromised attack to download the Winrar archiving tool, the Winrar

00:13:36,870 --> 00:13:38,790

170
00:13:38,880 --> 00:13:39,120
Yeah.

00:03:07,580 --> 00:03:13,010

00:03:13,160 --> 00:03:20,960
So what happens is Rukh, uh, operators have started leveraging group policy shutdown scripts to deploy

55

their ransomware binary.

56

So what happens is so, uh, Rukh always targets on, uh, colonizing the complete network rather than

57

so that they have a foothold, a stronger foothold onto the system.

60
00:03:45,380 --> 00:03:49,070

61
00:03:49,070 --> 00:03:52,310
So they leverage this group policy shutdown scripts.

62
00:03:52,310 --> 00:03:58,760
So making it to even more worse condition because, uh, uh, so it is very impossible for us to stop

00:03:58,760 --> 00:04:00,350
in the execution phase.

00:04:00,350 --> 00:04:04,190

Uh, yeah, that is how the privilege is being escalated.

85
00:05:23,960 --> 00:05:26,930

86
00:05:26,930 --> 00:05:33,650
So when it comes to defense evasion, Rukh actually executes gmer, uh, for the defense evasion.

87
00:05:33,650 --> 00:05:38,240

00:05:38,690 --> 00:05:39,770
Uh, yeah.

89
00:05:39,770 --> 00:05:47,870
So what happens is Jima, if you execute Jima, it has a list of all the running processes, um, all

the, even the hidden running processes in this.

91
00:05:50,060 --> 00:05:57,290
So Jima is actually a defensive tool that was developed to kill the rootkits from the system, like

92

the unwanted or malicious programs or unwanted applications from the system.

00:06:03,620 --> 00:06:11,120
But we have seen threat actors leveraging this Jima to find out the antiviruses that are running, to

94

95
00:06:15,950 --> 00:06:20,240
So it it yeah, it can use it as a defense evasion.

00:06:20,720 --> 00:06:21,200
Yeah.

97
00:06:21,200 --> 00:06:22,610
So that is how so.

the process and to kill it.

00:06:43,460 --> 00:06:46,070

group.

So what happens?

Rukh is found to use wake on LAN concept for sending a network message to all the devices in the network

00:10:07,740 --> 00:10:09,210

155
00:10:20,670 --> 00:10:25,230

156
00:10:25,290 --> 00:10:26,610
Um, see share.

157
00:10:26,610 --> 00:10:31,500
You all know admin share and begins to encrypt the file and place the ransom note.

158
00:10:31,920 --> 00:10:38,850
So this is like, yeah, so this, uh, wake on LAN is like a Ethernet or a token ring.

159
00:10:38,880 --> 00:10:42,570
Okay, That, uh, it's, it's like a computer networking standard.

160
00:10:42,570 --> 00:10:50,820
So it is just, it sends a networking packet or a networking message to all the hosts inside the network

161
00:10:50,820 --> 00:10:53,790
to make them awake from the, uh, sleep mode.

162
00:10:53,790 --> 00:10:57,090
So this is what have given example.

00:10:57,800 --> 00:10:59,240
In the image.

00:11:00,020 --> 00:11:00,470

Yeah.

Rook leverages, uh, the leverages dot x to grant permission to modify the folder.

00:11:25,400 --> 00:11:31,670

00:11:31,670 --> 00:11:35,150
You can run it and you can give permission to a folder.

00:11:35,350 --> 00:11:42,620
Uh, whether you can modify it, uh, whether you can read it, write it, modify it, alter it, All

the permissions are being granted by Calles.

So to make sure whether they.

00:11:48,320 --> 00:11:51,350
Yeah, they encrypt the file very correctly.

179
00:11:51,350 --> 00:11:55,430
So they leverages this dot X to grant permission.

180
00:11:55,850 --> 00:11:56,570
Uh, this is what.

uh, monitoring tools, the antivirus.

00:05:37,680 --> 00:05:38,100

00:05:41,400 --> 00:05:47,520

when I put my system and if I start my system, it directly takes me as an administrator log on.

115
00:07:15,870 --> 00:07:21,630
So there will be always a plain main binary that gets executed everything.

116
00:07:21,630 --> 00:07:26,220
So lockbit usually delete itself from the disk.

117
00:07:26,220 --> 00:07:34,020
And other thing is it will also execute files and the associated binary files or anything by an associated

118
00:07:34,020 --> 00:07:36,600
script so it does not directly execute it.

119
00:07:36,600 --> 00:07:43,320
For example, if it is PowerShell dot exe or if it is a notepad or it does not execute it directly,

120
00:07:43,320 --> 00:07:48,990
it will obfuscate it, encode it, and then it will run it so that it acts as a defense.

121
00:07:48,990 --> 00:07:51,960
So the next stage is the credential access stage.

122
00:07:51,960 --> 00:07:59,430
In this credential access stage Lockbit often use use the Procdump, which is a one of the internal

123
00:07:59,820 --> 00:08:02,260
to dump the credential from Lsass directly.

124
00:08:02,260 --> 00:08:04,090
So your know Mimikatz is being used.

125
00:08:04,090 --> 00:08:07,840
It uses the procdump sysinternals to get the content.

126
00:08:07,840 --> 00:08:08,040
Yeah.

127
00:08:08,050 --> 00:08:10,750
To dump the file out of else.

128
00:08:12,160 --> 00:08:14,080
So the next stage is the discovery stage.

129
00:08:14,080 --> 00:08:20,710
So in discovery stage it will we have found it executing multiple discovery commands such as the net

130
00:08:20,710 --> 00:08:27,070
commands and test commands, domain test commands, all these commands to take the detail to discover

131
00:08:27,070 --> 00:08:32,830
about the host name, the host configuration domain configurations.

132
00:08:33,160 --> 00:08:34,240
Domain trust.

133
00:08:34,780 --> 00:08:37,510
All these are local drive configuration, everything.

134
00:08:37,510 --> 00:08:43,390
And when it comes to the network, when it has to discover about the network, we have seen it using

135
00:08:43,480 --> 00:08:49,870
a new software called Soft Perfect Network Scanner, which is used to scan the target network.

136
00:08:49,870 --> 00:08:58,240
So this is also similar to that of the advanced port scanner that scans the network completely.

137
00:08:58,240 --> 00:09:03,430
So these are the discovery things that log exec uses for lateral movement.

138
00:09:03,430 --> 00:09:08,560
Lockbit uses Splashtop, the most known remote desktop software.

139
00:09:08,680 --> 00:09:15,050
So it uses a lateral movement to laterally move inside the environment after discovering it, right

140
00:09:15,050 --> 00:09:21,860
after discovering what are the next hops, it will use the lateral splashtop to laterally move inside

141
00:09:22,490 --> 00:09:24,800
and the next stage is the command and control.

142
00:09:24,800 --> 00:09:30,800
In this stage of command and control, it uses P link to automate action on Windows.

143
00:09:30,800 --> 00:09:38,600
So p link is nothing, but it is like putty, but it is a command line utility of putty I would say.

144
00:09:38,810 --> 00:09:39,110
Yeah.

145
00:09:39,110 --> 00:09:46,010
So in command line you can like putty, you can start to initiate a connection with the SSH using your

146
00:09:46,010 --> 00:09:46,880
credentials.

147
00:09:46,880 --> 00:09:50,450
But it so in putty we do it in a different way, right?

148
00:09:50,450 --> 00:09:55,070
But in P link we make it in a command line utility.

149
00:09:55,340 --> 00:09:56,600
That is how so.

150
00:09:56,600 --> 00:10:00,260
Only through all file transfers everything is being happening.

151
00:10:00,260 --> 00:10:02,600
So command and control is through.

152
00:10:02,660 --> 00:10:03,500
SSH.

153
00:10:04,010 --> 00:10:05,570
Next stage is the exfiltration.

154
00:10:05,570 --> 00:10:14,060
So in exfiltration we have seen Lockbit using publicly available file sharing services also for to exfiltrate,

155
00:10:14,060 --> 00:10:16,640
but the most commonly used is the R clone.

156
00:10:16,640 --> 00:10:22,490
As we all know, it's a open source command line cloud storage manager.

157
00:10:22,490 --> 00:10:28,160
It is being used like all other ransomware and mega is the same like all other ransomware also uses

158
00:10:28,160 --> 00:10:31,190
the mega that is publicly available file sharing service.

159
00:10:31,190 --> 00:10:34,640
So these are the things that have been seen in exfiltration.

160
00:10:34,640 --> 00:10:37,250
So the last stage is the impact stage.

161
00:10:37,250 --> 00:10:48,260
So in this stage, Lockbit tries to like remove or clear all the identity of their behaviors by deleting

162
00:10:48,260 --> 00:10:49,160
their logs.

163
00:10:49,160 --> 00:10:56,330
They empties the recycle bin and also they delete the volume shadow copy, which contains the details

164
00:10:56,330 --> 00:10:57,170
about the backup.

165
00:10:57,170 --> 00:10:57,560
Right?

166
00:10:57,560 --> 00:11:03,800
So it deletes everything and it also terminates all the processes and services that it has done because

167
00:11:03,800 --> 00:11:07,130
all the other stages have been achieved, right?

168
00:11:07,130 --> 00:11:14,060
So now there is no need for all other processes or anything, so it deletes everything to, to never

169
00:11:14,060 --> 00:11:15,290
leave a trace of it.

170
00:11:15,290 --> 00:11:19,310
And finally it will encrypt all the data from the mission.

171
00:11:19,850 --> 00:11:20,270
Yeah.

172
00:11:20,270 --> 00:11:26,000
So and leaving an extension called dot log bit and putting a ransom note.

173
00:11:26,870 --> 00:11:34,400
This is how like this is the attack pattern of log bit that is most commonly seen and have added an

174
00:11:34,400 --> 00:11:40,520
image for a sample where in this image you can clearly see that brute force attack or use of stolen

175
00:11:40,520 --> 00:11:44,330
credentials that is by and VPN access as I already told.

176
00:11:44,330 --> 00:11:44,690
Right.

177
00:11:44,690 --> 00:11:50,530
So yeah, stolen credentials also might be available sometime on or sent right on or.

178
00:11:50,570 --> 00:11:50,720
Yeah.

179
00:11:51,170 --> 00:11:54,230
So with that we can gain the access.

180
00:11:54,230 --> 00:11:58,280
And also they have also told unpatched vulnerability and system misconfiguration.

181
00:11:58,280 --> 00:12:05,510
This is why I told like as it is a ransomware, as a service it can deploy any techniques because it

182
00:12:05,510 --> 00:12:10,850
has all the vast variety of opportunity to share their techniques, right?

183
00:12:10,850 --> 00:12:15,420
So it depends on the victim's mission so that they can, yeah.

184
00:12:15,590 --> 00:12:19,790
Choose their initial access or any stage it can be.

185
00:12:19,790 --> 00:12:26,810
So then comes the initial access into the victim network after abusing VPN RDP Or it can be unpatched

186
00:12:26,810 --> 00:12:29,750
vulnerability or security misconfiguration.

187
00:12:29,750 --> 00:12:33,620
Then comes the command and control cobalt strike Metasploit.

188
00:12:33,650 --> 00:12:38,330
Then it will enumerate and laterally move, and then it encrypts the complete file system.

189
00:12:39,250 --> 00:12:42,670
This is the complete attack pattern of Lockbit.

190
00:12:42,700 --> 00:12:44,440
Hope you enjoyed this session.
